content.explanation:
🔗 Links — What Makes the Web a 'Web'

Remember from Lesson 1: HTML stands for HYPERTEXT Markup Language. Links are the 'hypertext' part — they're what connect one page to another, turning a pile of separate documents into an actual interconnected web. Without links, you couldn't click from Google's homepage to a search result. Every website is just a collection of pages stitched together by links.

Links are created with the <a> tag — short for 'anchor,' because historically it 'anchors' to another point of content.

🖼️ Images — Self-Closing Elements

This is your FIRST encounter with a self-closing tag: <img>. Remember in Lesson 2 we mentioned some elements don't wrap around content — images are exactly that case. An image doesn't have inner text content to wrap around, so there's no closing </img> tag. Instead, ALL the information (which picture to show, backup text, etc.) is given through attributes inside the single tag.

🆚 Why Needs a Closing Tag But Doesn't

This confuses almost everyone at first, so here's the actual reason: a link <a> needs visible, clickable text or content INSIDE it — that's what the user sees and clicks. An image has nothing to 'wrap around' — the picture itself isn't typed as text content, it's loaded from a file referenced in an attribute. No inner content needed means no closing tag needed.

🧭 Relative vs Absolute Paths — A Critical Real-World Skill

When you link to an image or another page, you give it a 'path' — basically directions to find that file. There are two types:

  • Absolute path — the FULL web address, e.g. https://example.com/photo.jpg. Used for linking to external websites.
  • Relative path — directions FROM your current file, e.g. images/photo.jpg. Used for files within your own project, like images you uploaded yourself.

This matters enormously once you start building real projects: if you use the wrong type of path, your images will work perfectly on your own computer but break completely once deployed to GitHub Pages, Netlify, or Vercel — a frustrating bug nearly every beginner hits at least once.
content.syntax_breakdown:
🧱 Anatomy of a Link

<a href="https://google.com">Visit Google</a>
  • <a> — opening tag, short for 'anchor'
  • href — attribute name, short for 'hypertext reference.' This is THE required attribute for a link — without it, your <a> tag does nothing clickable at all.
  • "https://google.com" — the attribute value: the destination address
  • Visit Google — the visible, clickable text content (this is what shows up underlined/colored on the page)
  • </a> — closing tag

🔓 Opening Links in a New Tab

<a href="https://google.com" target="_blank">Visit Google</a>

Adding target="_blank" tells the browser to open this link in a brand new tab instead of navigating away from your current page. This is good practice for external links so visitors don't lose your site entirely.

🧱 Anatomy of an Image

<img src="cat.jpg" alt="A sleeping orange cat">
  • <img> — the self-closing tag, no separate closing tag needed
  • src — short for 'source.' This is the path to the actual image file. Required — without it, there's no image to display.
  • alt — short for 'alternative text.' This is a written description of the image that displays if the image fails to load, AND is read aloud by screen readers for visually impaired users. This attribute is required for accessibility and should always be meaningful, never left blank or skipped.

🧭 Path Examples Explained

<!-- Absolute path: points to a file on another website -->
<img src="https://example.com/images/cat.jpg" alt="A cat">

<!-- Relative path: points to a file inside YOUR project folder -->
<img src="images/cat.jpg" alt="A cat">

<!-- Relative path: file in the SAME folder as your HTML file -->
<img src="cat.jpg" alt="A cat">

The relative path is read like folder directions on your phone: 'go into the images folder, then find cat.jpg.' Get this wrong (wrong folder name, wrong file name, wrong capitalization) and the browser simply shows a broken image icon.
content.common_mistakes:
⚠️ Common Beginner Mistakes

  • Forgetting href on a link — <a>Click here</a> with no href does nothing when clicked.
  • Forgetting alt on an image — breaks accessibility for screen reader users and leaves nothing to display if the image fails to load.
  • Mismatched capitalization in file paths — Cat.jpg vs cat.jpg are different files on many servers (including GitHub Pages and Netlify), even though they might look the same to you.
  • Using an absolute path when you meant a relative one — pointing to a file on your own computer with a path like C:/Users/You/cat.jpg will only work on YOUR device, and will be a broken image for everyone else once deployed.

editor_sandbox.starter_code:
My Favorite Sites

Here are some links I use often.

mini_challenge.instruction:
🎯 Challenge:

1. Add a link to https://github.com with the visible text: My GitHub
2. Make the link open in a new tab
3. Below it, add a paragraph that says: links connect the web together
4. Run and verify your code
mini_challenge.hint:
👉 Example: My GitHub

links connect the web together

quiz_engine.question: Why doesn't the tag have a separate closing tag like ?
options: Because images are not real HTML elements
Because it has no inner content to wrap around — all info is given via attributes
Because closing tags are optional for all elements
quiz_engine.explanation_feedback: Correct 🎉 is self-closing because there's no text content to wrap — everything (which file, backup text) is passed through attributes like src and alt.

takeaways: <a> creates links — href is the required attribute pointing to the destination
target="_blank" opens a link in a new tab
is self-closing — no closing tag needed
src points to the image file; alt is required descriptive backup text
Absolute paths are full web addresses; relative paths point to files within your own project
Wrong paths are a top cause of broken images/links after deployment

cheat_sheet.target=\"_blank\": Opens link in a new tab
cheat_sheet.alt attribute: Required backup text + accessibility for images
